label: Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space
definition: A Hilbert space of functions in which evaluation at any point is a continuous operation realized by an inner product with a kernel function, the mathematical structure that makes the kernel trick rigorous and underlies support vector machines and Gaussian processes.

body.text: A reproducing kernel Hilbert space, or RKHS, is a special kind of hilbert-space whose elements are functions and in which pointwise evaluation is well behaved. In a generic space of functions, the value of a function at a single point can be a discontinuous, ill-mannered operation; in an RKHS it is not. The defining property is that for every input point there is a particular function in the space, called the kernel section at that point, such that evaluating any function at that point is the same as taking its inner product with that kernel section. This is the reproducing property: the inner product reproduces function values. The two-argument function that assembles all these sections, the kernel, is symmetric and positive-definite, and the Moore-Aronszajn theorem guarantees that every such kernel corresponds to exactly one RKHS and vice versa. The kernel and the space are two views of the same object.

The reason this structure is so useful in machine learning is that it makes the kernel trick rigorous. A positive-definite kernel can be written as an inner product of feature maps in the RKHS: comparing two inputs with the kernel is exactly comparing their (often infinite-dimensional) feature representations by inner product, without ever forming those representations explicitly. This is what lets a kernel-method operate in a vast or infinite feature space at the cost of evaluating a simple kernel function on the original inputs. A linear method, expressed entirely in terms of inner products, is thereby lifted into a powerful nonlinear method simply by swapping the ordinary dot product for a kernel, with all the heavy lifting hidden inside the geometry of the RKHS.

The bridge from this abstract space to a concrete finite computation is the representer theorem. It states that the function minimizing a typical regularized learning objective, data-fit plus a penalty on the RKHS norm, always lies in the finite-dimensional subspace spanned by the kernel sections at the training points, no matter how infinite-dimensional the full space is. The optimal predictor is therefore a finite weighted sum of kernel evaluations against the training data, so an infinite-dimensional optimization collapses to solving for one coefficient per training example. This is precisely why the support-vector-machine, kernel ridge regression, and kernel principal-component-analysis are tractable: the representer theorem reduces them to convex-optimization problems in finitely many variables. The RKHS norm in the penalty controls smoothness, formalizing the bias toward simple functions that keeps these methods from overfitting.

The RKHS viewpoint reaches well beyond classical kernel machines. Gaussian processes are the probabilistic twin of kernel methods, with the covariance function playing the role of the kernel and the RKHS describing the functions the prior favors. The maximum mean embedding maps an entire probability distribution to a single point in an RKHS, giving a distance between distributions (the maximum mean discrepancy) that powers two-sample tests and some generative-model objectives. The choice of kernel encodes prior structure: a translation-invariant kernel has, by Bochner's theorem, a spectral description via the fourier-transform, linking the smoothness of functions in the space to a frequency distribution, and recent work connects wide neural networks to specific kernels (the neural tangent kernel) and so to specific reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces. Across all of these, the RKHS is the rigorous container that turns "compare things by a kernel" into a precise geometry of functions, the mathematical foundation on which kernel methods stand.
